summary:
The Islamic Conquest of Spain (711–718 AD) was accomplished with remarkable speed when Umayyad forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed the Strait of Gibraltar (named after him: Jebel Tariq, 'Mountain of Tariq') from North Africa with approximately 7,000 Berber troops, defeated the last [[event:2039|Visigothic]] king Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete, and within seven years controlled most of the Iberian Peninsula. The Visigoths offered surprisingly little resistance — partly due to internal political divisions and recent civil war, partly because many of the Jewish and Roman-descended population had no particular loyalty to their Arian Visigothic rulers.

The resulting Muslim state in Iberia (al-Andalus) would last in various forms for nearly 800 years, until the [[event:2209|Reconquista]] was completed in 1492. At its height under the [[event:2192|Cordoba Caliphate]], al-Andalus was the most sophisticated civilization in Europe — with advanced agriculture (introducing rice, oranges, and sugarcane), flourishing cities (Cordoba was the largest city in Europe with 500,000+ inhabitants), and extraordinary intellectual life in which Muslim, Jewish, and Christian scholars collaborated. The [[event:2193|Great Mosque of Cordoba]] remains one of the architectural masterpieces of the medieval world. The conquest halted at the Pyrenees when Frankish forces under Charles Martel defeated a raiding party at the [[event:2132|Battle of Tours]] (732 AD), establishing the basic geographic boundary between Muslim and Christian Europe.
